one_line_definition: Method of measuring elastic modulus by tapping a rod, exciting a longitudinal standing wave, and measuring the resonant frequency with a microphone.
Material property relating stress to strain in uniaxial loading; calculated from resonant frequency and density in acoustic methods.
A standing wave in which particle displacement is parallel to the wave propagation direction; in a free rod the fundamental has nodes at the midpoint and antinodes at the ends.
Method of combining individual measurement uncertainties (by differentiation or quadrature addition) to estimate the total uncertainty in a derived quantity.
Resonant modes in which particle motion is perpendicular to the rod axis; lower frequency than longitudinal modes for slender rods; used for elevated-temperature measurements.

aliases_seen: standing wave measurement of interfaces and layers, continuous-wave ultrasonics
one_line_definition: Ultrasonic measurement method using a frequency-swept continuous wave to create a superimposed standing wave in a component, enabling back-face measurement of surface layer properties.
Ratio of the RMS amplitude of the standing wave in the measurement condition to the reference (solid-air) condition; used to extract the interface reflection coefficient R.
Frequency at which constructive interference of all reflections maximises the standing wave amplitude; fs = c/(2L) for the fundamental mode.
Product of material density and speed of sound (z = ρc); determines the fraction of an ultrasonic wave reflected at an interface.
Model treating a thin interface layer as a spring of stiffness K; reflection coefficient depends on K and wave frequency, valid when layer thickness is much less than acoustic wavelength.
Effective stiffness of an interface layer (thin film, rough contact) that determines the proportion of ultrasound reflected; K = B/h for a liquid layer of bulk modulus B and thickness h.
Material property quantifying the exponential decay of ultrasonic wave amplitude with distance (A = A₀ e^{−αL}).
Method using shear-polarised ultrasound to measure liquid viscosity from the complex acoustic impedance at a solid-liquid interface.
Use of ultrasonic wave velocity or resonant frequency shift in a bolt to measure applied tensile load without direct access to the bolt interior.

aliases_seen: sampled-continuous-wave technique, frequency-tracked gated rf pulses
one_line_definition: Ultrasonic spectrum analysis method in which the RF drive frequency is slaved to the spectrum analyser's sweep frequency, eliminating finite-pulse-width modulation artefacts.
Analysis of ultrasonic echoes in the frequency domain (via Fourier transform) to measure material velocity, attenuation, and flaw characteristics.
Ultrasonic receiver that outputs a DC signal proportional to total acoustic flux regardless of phase; works with frequency-tracking but not broadband shock excitation.
Temporal windowing of ultrasonic echoes before spectrum analysis; gate width and position affect conventional analysis but not frequency-tracking results.

aliases_seen: multiple wave method, continuous-wave standing wave technique
one_line_definition: Variant of the standing wave technique in which successive reflections amplify the influence of the interface reflection coefficient, enabling measurement of thinner and thicker films than single-pulse methods.
Measurement of the thickness of a thin oil layer at an interface using the ultrasonic spring model or standing wave method; effective range roughly 0.5–15 µm for spring model.
Shift in wave phase upon reflection at an interface; ranges from 0 (thick film, K→0) to π/2 (thin film, K→∞); governs the resonant frequency condition in standing wave methods.
Superposition of waves of equal frequency travelling in opposite directions; produces standing wave maxima (antinodes) and minima (nodes).
Technique using the acoustic resonance spectrum of a component (particularly cylindrical) to characterise its material properties or detect defects; referenced by title only in the PREVIEW file.
